narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 37.50—slightly above the typical 36.70 average—and an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, which is a bit below the usual 3.21. However, your Year 1 school decile of 3 and Year 3 decile of 2 indicate performance in the lower third of your peers, compared to averages around the middle of the pack. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 49.6% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—a risk high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which signaled to the model a notable dip in academic standing relative to others at a critical stage. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your LSAT score, the second-biggest influence, which pulled strongly in the direction of passing by showing strong reasoning and test-taking ability above the norm. Your undergraduate GPA came third in impact, offering a moderate positive nudge toward success but not enough to offset the concerns from later school performance. Overall, while your LSAT and GPA suggested potential, the model weighed your lower Year 3 decile as the top driver of risk, placing you below the typical successful applicant who maintains steadier mid-to-high rankings throughout. This isn't a judgment on your character or background—many from similar circumstances thrive—but a data-driven call based on patterns where such deciles correlate with bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your recent academic deciles, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted prep that addresses any Year 3 challenges. Strengthening that area could flip the model's view on your bar odds. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews or appeal with evidence of improvement since Year 3. Keep pushing—your LSAT strength shows you're capable, and with focused steps, stronger opportunities await.
